concepts.name: Mathematical Definition
concepts.intuition_gloss: The exact wording of what a term means is the whole game — a definition is a contract you must be able to unfold on demand.
concepts.formal_gloss: A stipulation introducing a new term as an abbreviation for a precise condition, so every use of the term can be replaced by that condition in proofs.

resources.title: Theorem — Wikipedia (terminology of definitions, theorems, proofs)

sources: standard transition-to-proof curricula (Book of Proof, Velleman's How to Prove It)
Princeton Companion I.3 (Some Fundamental Mathematical Definitions)

concepts.name: Conjecture
concepts.intuition_gloss: A pattern you've spotted and believe is always true, stated precisely and put on trial — mathematics grows by making and testing such guesses.
concepts.formal_gloss: A precisely stated proposition believed true on the basis of evidence or heuristics but not yet proved; it becomes a theorem when proved or is refuted by counterexample.

resources.title: Mathematics and Plausible Reasoning (Polya) — Wikipedia overview

concepts.name: Counterexample
concepts.intuition_gloss: One black swan kills 'all swans are white' — a single well-chosen example demolishes a universal claim for good.
concepts.formal_gloss: An object satisfying the hypotheses of a universally quantified statement but violating its conclusion, thereby disproving the statement.

resources.title: Book of Proof (Hammack), ch. on disproof

sources: Lakatos, Proofs and Refutations (counterexample-driven concept refinement)

concepts.name: Theorem, Lemma & Corollary
concepts.intuition_gloss: Mathematical knowledge comes packaged as if-then statements of different sizes: workhorse lemmas feed headline theorems, which drop corollaries for free.
concepts.formal_gloss: A theorem is a proved statement, typically of the form hypotheses imply conclusion; a lemma is a proved auxiliary step, and a corollary a near-immediate consequence.
sources: Princeton Companion I.2 (The Language and Grammar of Mathematics)

concepts.name: Direct Proof
concepts.intuition_gloss: The straight road: start from what you're given, unfold the definitions, and walk step by logical step until you arrive at the claim.
concepts.formal_gloss: A derivation of a conditional P implies Q by assuming P and reaching Q through a chain of valid inferences from definitions, axioms, and prior results.

concepts.name: Proof by Contrapositive
concepts.intuition_gloss: Instead of proving 'if it rains, the ground gets wet', prove 'if the ground is dry, it didn't rain' — the same claim attacked from the other end.
concepts.formal_gloss: A proof of P implies Q obtained by directly proving the logically equivalent statement not-Q implies not-P.

concepts.name: Proof by Cases
concepts.intuition_gloss: Split the world into a complete set of scenarios — even or odd, positive or negative — and win each battle separately to win the war.
concepts.formal_gloss: A proof that establishes a statement by partitioning all possibilities into finitely many exhaustive cases and proving the statement in each case.
sources: four-color theorem as canonical large-scale exemplar

concepts.name: Constructive vs. Non-constructive Proof
concepts.intuition_gloss: You can prove treasure exists by digging it up, or by showing the map forces it to be somewhere — only the first hands you the treasure.
concepts.formal_gloss: A constructive proof of an existential statement exhibits or algorithmically produces a witness; a non-constructive proof establishes existence without providing one, often via contradiction or counting.
sources: Princeton Companion IV.22 (Logic and Model Theory context)

concepts.name: Without Loss of Generality
concepts.intuition_gloss: When several scenarios are mirror images of each other, prove just one and let symmetry stamp the result onto the rest.
concepts.formal_gloss: A proof step restricting attention to one case among several that are equivalent under a symmetry of the problem, the other cases following by the same argument mutatis mutandis.
sources: standard proof-writing conventions (Velleman, Book of Proof)

concepts.name: Polya's Problem-Solving Cycle
concepts.intuition_gloss: Polya's four-beat rhythm for any hard problem: truly understand it, devise a plan, carry the plan out, then look back at what worked.
concepts.formal_gloss: Polya's framework of heuristic phases — understanding the problem, devising a plan, carrying out the plan, and looking back — with named heuristics (analogy, specialization, auxiliary problems) attached to each phase.

concepts.name: Working Backwards
concepts.intuition_gloss: Stand at the finish line and ask 'what would get me here?', walking the maze in reverse until you meet the entrance.
concepts.formal_gloss: The heuristic of reasoning from the desired conclusion to sufficient intermediate conditions until reaching the given hypotheses, then presenting the argument in forward order.
sources: Polya, How to Solve It (working backwards / Pappus's analysis)

concepts.name: Trying Small Cases
concepts.intuition_gloss: Before wrestling the general beast, play with the tiniest examples — n equals 1, 2, 3 — and let the pattern introduce itself.
concepts.formal_gloss: The heuristic of instantiating a general problem at small or extreme parameter values to generate data, form conjectures, and reveal structure that guides the general argument.

concepts.name: Generalization & Abstraction
concepts.intuition_gloss: Strip away what's incidental until different problems reveal the same skeleton — then solve the skeleton once, everywhere.
concepts.formal_gloss: The process of extracting the essential structure common to specific instances into a general definition or axiom system whose theorems apply to every instance at once.

resources.title: The Princeton Companion to Mathematics — I.4 The General Goals of Mathematical Research

sources: history of the axiomatic method (Hilbert, Bourbaki)

concepts.name: Reasoning by Analogy
concepts.intuition_gloss: Borrow the shape of a solved problem to attack an unsolved one — spheres are to space what circles are to the plane.
concepts.formal_gloss: The heuristic of transferring conjectures, methods, or structures between domains that share partial structural correspondence, generating plausible but unproven claims to be verified.
sources: Polya, Mathematics and Plausible Reasoning vol. 1 (Induction and Analogy in Mathematics)

concepts.name: Reduction to a Known Problem
concepts.intuition_gloss: The mathematician's oldest trick: don't solve the problem in front of you — turn it into one somebody already solved.
concepts.formal_gloss: Solving problem A by exhibiting a transformation carrying instances of A to instances of an already-solved problem B and mapping B's solutions back to A.
sources: Polya, How to Solve It (auxiliary problems)

concepts.name: Invariants & Monovariants
concepts.intuition_gloss: Find something the allowed moves can never change — if the goal state has a different value of it, the goal is provably unreachable.
concepts.formal_gloss: A quantity or property preserved (invariant) or monotonically changed (monovariant) by every permitted operation, used to prove impossibility or termination.

resources.title: Problem-Solving Strategies (Engel), ch. 1: The Invariance Principle

concepts.name: Extremal Principle
concepts.intuition_gloss: Grab the biggest, smallest, or closest object in the problem — extremes are cornered animals, and their special position forces conclusions.
concepts.formal_gloss: The heuristic of examining an element that is extremal with respect to some ordering, whose maximality or minimality yields constraints, often combined with well-ordering or contradiction.

resources.title: Problem-Solving Strategies (Engel), ch. 3: The Extremal Principle

concepts.name: Exploiting Symmetry
concepts.intuition_gloss: When a problem looks the same after a swap, rotation, or relabeling, that sameness is free information — use it to halve the work or force equalities.
concepts.formal_gloss: The strategy of using a problem's automorphisms — transformations preserving its data — to reduce cases, constrain solutions, or average over orbits.
sources: Polya, How to Solve It (symmetry heuristic)

concepts.name: Multiple Representations
concepts.intuition_gloss: The same idea can be a picture, a formula, a table, or a story — power comes from hopping to whichever view makes the problem easy.
concepts.formal_gloss: The skill of encoding one mathematical object in several equivalent registers (symbolic, graphical, numerical, verbal) and translating between them to expose different affordances.
sources: mathematics-education research (Lesh, Duval on registers of representation)

concepts.name: Visual & Diagrammatic Reasoning
concepts.intuition_gloss: Draw the thing — a good picture can carry an entire argument, turning an algebraic identity into something you simply see.
concepts.formal_gloss: The use of diagrams and spatial structure as cognitive scaffolding for conjecture and proof, from proofs-without-words to commutative diagrams, with the obligation that pictures be backed by rigorous argument.

concepts.name: Estimation & Sanity Checking
concepts.intuition_gloss: Before trusting an answer, weigh it on rough scales — check units, extreme cases, and orders of magnitude to catch nonsense early.
concepts.formal_gloss: The practice of bounding a quantity by decomposing it into estimable factors and testing results against limiting cases, dimensional constraints, and known benchmarks.

concepts.name: Mathematical Notation
concepts.intuition_gloss: Good notation is a lever: it packs an idea into marks that think for you, the way positional digits made arithmetic easy.
concepts.formal_gloss: The system of conventional symbols and syntax for denoting mathematical objects and operations, whose design (compactness, suggestiveness, unambiguity) materially affects reasoning.
sources: Princeton Companion I.2 (The Language and Grammar of Mathematics)

concepts.name: Reading & Writing Proofs
concepts.intuition_gloss: A proof is a piece of writing aimed at a human reader — learning to read one slowly and write one clearly is a craft of its own.
concepts.formal_gloss: The conventions for communicating proofs — statement-proof structure, quantifier discipline, appropriate granularity of steps — and the skill of verifying and reconstructing others' arguments.

resources.title: Mathematical Writing (Knuth, Larrabee, Roberts) — course notes
On writing — Terence Tao's advice pages

concepts.name: Metacognitive Monitoring
concepts.intuition_gloss: The inner supervisor who asks 'is this approach working? do I actually understand this step?' — expert problem solvers audit themselves constantly.
concepts.formal_gloss: The executive-control component of problem solving — planning, progress monitoring, and strategy switching — identified by Schoenfeld as a chief determinant of problem-solving success beyond resource knowledge.

resources.title: How to Solve It (Polya) — Wikipedia ('looking back')

concepts.name: Rigor & Intuition
concepts.intuition_gloss: Mathematicians run on fast intuition checked by slow rigor — mastery is when the two agree, and your gut speaks fluent epsilon-delta.
concepts.formal_gloss: The interplay between informal heuristic understanding and formal deductive verification, often described as Tao's three stages: pre-rigorous, rigorous, and post-rigorous practice.

resources.title: There's more to mathematics than rigour and proofs — Terence Tao
On Proof and Progress in Mathematics — W. Thurston (arXiv)

sources: Thurston, On Proof and Progress in Mathematics (1994)

concepts.name: Formalization & Proof Assistants
concepts.intuition_gloss: Write a proof so precisely that a computer can verify every step — turning mathematical certainty into a compile check.
concepts.formal_gloss: The translation of informal proofs into a formal logical calculus checked by software (proof assistants such as Lean, Coq, Isabelle), with libraries like mathlib formalizing large bodies of mathematics.
